items.rationale: His core thesis: control (not 'ongoing efforts') is what creates the trust dependencies and information asymmetries securities laws target; removing control is what makes blockchains worthwhile and earns lighter regulation.
items.age_note: Evolved over 18mo from broad pro-decentralization (2023-12) to a sharpened control-based reframe by early 2025; current stance is the refined version.

citations.quote: Decentralization should mean the absence of control, not the suppression of ongoing efforts. It's time to fix it.
Decentralization still matters.
Decentralization eliminates control, but it needs to be incentivized.

items.rationale: Wants tokens whose value accrues from autonomous blockchain systems (not centralized teams); sees genuine value accrual as the only sustainable alternative to memecoins.

citations.quote: Network tokens should have real economic value. It's the only way forward.
it's time to focus on crypto's purpose: decentralization.

items.rationale: Sees the DUNA as a breakthrough giving DAOs legal existence, limited liability and tax capability without compromising decentralization; calls adoption a strategic imperative after the CA partnership-liability ruling.

citations.quote: It's now a strategic imperative for DAO's to use legal wrappers. The...(DUNA) is one of the best options available.
the DUNA provides a great tool for startups here.

items.rationale: Post-election he sees a bipartisan path to clear rules ending regulation-by-enforcement; views legislation as the durable fix that levels the playing field.
items.age_note: Sharp shift from years of adversarial posture (2023-2024) to optimism after the 2024 election and SEC's retreat.

citations.quote: more optimistic than I've ever been that crypto is going to get regulatory clarity this year.
The weaponization of regulatory uncertainty by the SEC is at an end. Now it's Congress' turn.

items.rationale: Excited for low/no-fee cross-border payments; insists stablecoins are dollar equivalents not investment products, and opposes yield-bearing models that incentivize risk-taking.

citations.quote: low to no-fee cross border transactions with stablecoins
Stablecoins aren't investment products. They're dollar equivalents and should have a similar risk profile.

items.rationale: Long argued frontends/DEXs aren't brokers; treats the Risley v. Uniswap holding as a Section-230-like foundation for autonomous, credibly neutral systems.

citations.quote: the Risley v. Uniswap holding is a boon for credibly neutral, decentralized and autonomous blockchain systems. Absolutely huge.
Most DEX frontends aren't brokers.

items.rationale: Sees NFTs as revolutionary for artists to monetize and bypass intermediaries; backing a Creator Defense Fund and pushing the SEC for an NFT safe harbor.

citations.quote: NFTs unlock incredible opportunities for creators. But regulatory uncertainty is stifling innovation and the most creative minds online.
NFTs enable digital property rights in art, music, tickets... This is revolutionary technology.

items.rationale: Names these as the use cases that will demonstrate the value of trustless tech and build political support; flags AI tokens as future network tokens once agents move onchain.

citations.quote: use cases like decentralized messaging, social and AI are all going to be key toward building broader support in DC.
once AI agents move onchain, I think they'd be more like network tokens.

items.rationale: Expects traditional securities to move onchain in 2025 and thinks the whole industry should want it — provided securities laws still apply to those tokenized instruments.

citations.quote: Everyone in this industry should want the traditional securities market onchain.
The UK issuing treasuries directly onchain would be a massive unlock for DeFi.

items.rationale: His most acute recent worry: VCs/companies launching tokens tied to off-chain businesses (or dropping any blockchain nexus) are unregistered securities that endanger both crypto's legitimacy and securities law itself.

citations.quote: dropping the pretense of tokens even having to relate to a blockchain system is the most damaging and dangerous step backwards
This is called an unregistered securities offering and the rules are actually quite clear.

items.rationale: Views purely speculative tokens as value-extraction that harms the industry's long-term standing; distinguishes community memecoins from the dominant scammy flood, and warns casino culture isn't a principled freedom argument.

citations.quote: Memecoins are the opposite of equity — ownership of nothing versus something.
Memecoins are the crypto equivalent of chain emails.

items.rationale: Believes fast insider liquidity breeds get-rich-quick schemes and vaporware (SPAC analogy); pushes 4yr/1yr lockups, dilutable token allocations, and locking insiders until decentralization.

citations.quote: Unfettered access to secondary market liquidity is an existential threat to innovation.
4 year/1 year cliff token lockups should be standard.

items.rationale: Argues offshore foundations bring operational complexity, weaker protections, tax risk and value-extraction; warns consultants are manufacturing false urgency to push US founders offshore.

citations.quote: There couldn't be a more glaringly obvious sign that we need to kill foreign foundation structures.
All the reasons they give (investor preference, doubts about US regulatory clarity, etc.) are nonsense. Be careful.

items.rationale: As the SEC turns constructive, he warns class-action lawyers will fill the enforcement vacuum and exploit unclear token law; a reason to still want SEC guidance and safe harbors.
items.age_note: Newly elevated in 2025 as the SEC threat receded; replaces the SEC as his top live legal-risk concern.

citations.quote: SEC may be constructive now, but plaintiffs lawyers certainly won't be.
Govern yourselves accordingly, or the plaintiffs' firms will.

items.rationale: Warns the industry is blurring TEEs (trusted hardware) with genuinely trustless systems, which he predicts will cause real harm.

citations.quote: The conflation of trusted execution environments with trustless blockchains is going to lead to a lot of pain.

items.rationale: Documents a16z portfolio companies losing bank accounts despite no added risk or illegality; treats it as illegal coordinated pressure (the Fed's approach 'illegal').
items.age_note: Acute in late 2024; expected to ease under the new administration, so live but declining.

citations.quote: Most debankings of @a16zcrypto portcos happened to pre-revenue and pre-token companies... No additional risks. No illegality. No justification.
The Fed's approach to crypto is illegal.

items.rationale: Dominant concern through 2023-2024 — calls it political 'lawfare' that exports uncertainty and offshores builders; by early 2025 he declares this weaponization ending.
items.age_note: Largely reversed: was his #1 concern (conviction ~95) for most of the corpus, but he calls it 'at an end' on 2025-02-21, so down-weighted to a residual/declining risk.

citations.quote: The vast majority of the industry just wants protection against the lawfare and unlawful actions being perpetrated by the current administration
The weaponization of regulatory uncertainty by the SEC is at an end.

items.rationale: Saw the rule as an unconstitutional attempt to strip decentralization from DeFi by stretching 'effectuate transactions'; predicted it wouldn't stand — and it was repealed.
items.age_note: Resolved: Senate repealed the rule 70-27 on 2025-03-04, so this is now a settled win rather than a live concern.

citations.quote: IRS is aiming to take the 'decentralization' out of DeFi... this new rule will also not stand.
Senate vote just repealed the harmful IRS broker rule that threatened DeFi

items.rationale: Prefers a level playing field over handouts; objects to special tax treatment for a single asset class and questions prioritizing a BTC reserve over the many fixes needed.

citations.quote: The law shouldn't be used to pick technologies winners and losers.
there are legitimate reasons to do the reserve. But the ranking of priorities matters

items.rationale: A California ruling that mere DAO participation can create partnership liability is, to him, a major blow to decentralized governance and a hostage-taking litigation vector.

citations.quote: Today, a California judge dealt a huge blow to decentralized governance.

items.rationale: His canonical example of a legitimate network token — value derived solely from a decentralized network, formally outside securities laws after ETF approvals; the model others should follow.

citations.quote: ETH is not a security, nor should transactions in ETH be securities transactions.
ETH (whose value is solely derived from a decentralized network)

items.rationale: Treats Bitcoin as the archetypal decentralized, uncontrolled network asset whose risk profile is commodity-like — excluded from securities laws.

citations.quote: If decentralization is the limiting principle of Howey, then transactions of Bitcoin are not violative of Howey.

items.rationale: Groups Solana with BTC/ETH as a network token with a pathway out of securities laws via decentralization (vs. centralized/company tokens).

citations.quote: Transactions of network tokens are likely to pass Howey where there's decentralization.

items.rationale: Sees it as a legitimately structured, non-rehypothecated, fiat-backed stablecoin distinguishable from yield offerings like UST; 'likely okay' under securities law.
items.age_note: Core 'USDC is likely okay' judgment stated 2023-12-29 and not restated since; treat as persisted (older view, never reversed).

citations.quote: USDC is likely okay, but yield bearing stablecoins should take note.

items.rationale: Purely speculative, 'ownership of nothing'; risk profile driven by information asymmetry and insider dumping. He accepts they're regulated differently but sees the launch trend as damaging.

citations.quote: Memecoins are the opposite of equity — ownership of nothing versus something.
True memecoins have no intrinsic value (no utility, no company, no anything) and their price is driven purely by speculation

items.rationale: His recurring example of a synthetic security — value derived solely from a centralized company (FTX), functionally a profits interest, which secondary trading should subject to securities laws.

citations.quote: FTT (whose value was solely derived from a centralized company, FTX)
secondary transactions of FTT should be subject to securities laws as the instrument was essentially a profits interest in FTX

risk_regime.rationale: As of Q1 2025 he is the most optimistic he has ever been: the existential regulatory fight is subsiding, the SEC has turned constructive, the IRS DeFi rule was repealed, and he expects market-structure clarity in 2025 making the US a top place to build. But his bullishness is structural and builder-oriented, not a price/speculation call — he is simultaneously and vocally bearish on the speculative excess (memecoins, company-backed tokens, fast insider liquidity) he thinks endangers the industry. Net posture: constructively risk-on on decentralized, value-accruing tech, with strong guardrails against the casino.

notes: Jennings is a16z crypto's head of policy/General Counsel; his 'market view' is overwhelmingly legal-regulatory and structural rather than price/trading-oriented, so 'risk posture' reflects optimism about the industry's regulatory trajectory and which token designs are legitimate, not portfolio positioning. Strongest recency-weighted signal: a hardened control-based decentralization framework (2025-02 onward) and a sharp pivot from anti-SEC 'lawfare' framing (2023-2024) to post-election optimism (Nov 2024-Mar 2025). Two stances were effectively resolved/reversed by the cutoff and down-weighted accordingly: SEC regulation-by-enforcement ('at an end,' 2025-02-21) and the IRS DeFi broker rule (repealed 2025-03-04); plaintiffs'-firm litigation has replaced the SEC as his top live legal risk. His central organizing distinction throughout is network tokens (good, decentralized, value from autonomous systems) vs. company-backed/synthetic-security tokens and memecoins (bad). Tokens are cited mainly as legal-category exemplars (ETH/BTC = decentralized commodities; FTT = synthetic security), not investment theses.
